status.state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
status.product_description: ICU patient monitoring dashboard aggregating data from ventilators, infusion pumps, and vital sign monitors. Real-time alerting with nurse station integration. HL7 FHIR compliant.

plan.description: Gather and document ICU stakeholder requirements: clinical workflows for nurses and intensivists, device integration scope (ventilators, infusion pumps, vital sign monitors), alerting escalation protocols, and nurse station notification flows. Produce a formal Business Requirements Document (BRD) and user story backlog.
acceptance_criteria: BRD reviewed and signed off by at least one clinical stakeholder
All device integration protocols documented (HL7 v2, HL7 FHIR, proprietary)
Alert escalation matrix defined with priority levels (P1-P4)
User story backlog covers all identified ICU workflows
Process flow diagrams include nurse station notification paths

plan.description: Conduct preliminary hazard analysis and FMEA for the ICU patient monitoring system per ISO 14971. Identify failure modes for data loss, alert suppression, erroneous readings, network outages, and device communication failures. Produce Hazard Identification Log, Risk Matrix (severity × probability), and initial Risk Control Measures.
acceptance_criteria: FMEA table covers all device types and communication channels
All critical hazards (patient harm potential) have documented risk controls
Risk matrix uses ISO 14971 severity × probability framework
Residual risk accepted or flagged for design mitigation before implementation
Safety engineer sign-off on hazard log completeness
ISO 14971 risk management file complete
Residual risk acceptable

plan.description: Map product to applicable regulatory frameworks: FDA 21 CFR Part 820 (QMS), IEC 62304 (software lifecycle), ISO 13485 (medical device QMS), HIPAA/HITECH (PHI handling), and HL7 FHIR compliance. Produce Standards Traceability Matrix linking each regulation to design controls and verification activities.

payload.classification: FDA Class II medical device software (SaMD)

acceptance_criteria: Every applicable clause from each standard mapped to a design control or artifact
Software safety class determined per IEC 62304 (Class B or C)
PHI data flows identified and mapped to HIPAA safeguard requirements
Regulatory strategy document approved by regulatory specialist
510(k) predicate device analysis completed
HIPAA risk assessment complete
BAA template prepared

plan.description: Draft legal artifacts for the ICU monitoring platform: HIPAA Business Associate Agreement (BAA) template, Terms of Service for hospital IT, Privacy Policy covering PHI handling, open-source license audit (SBOM pre-check), and IP review for HL7 FHIR integration libraries.

payload.phi_scope: real-time vital signs, device telemetry, patient identifiers

acceptance_criteria: HIPAA BAA template reviewed by legal counsel
Privacy Policy enumerates all PHI data categories collected
All third-party library licenses documented and compatible with commercial use
ToS covers liability limitations for clinical alert systems
IP review confirms no patent conflicts with HL7 FHIR implementation

plan.description: Design ICU dashboard UX: nurse station alert panel, multi-patient grid view, per-patient vital trend charts, device status indicators, and alert acknowledgment flows. Conduct accessibility audit (WCAG 2.1 AA). Produce high-fidelity wireframes and interactive prototype for clinical validation.
acceptance_criteria: Multi-patient grid displays ≥12 patients simultaneously without scrolling on 1920×1080
Critical alert acknowledgment requires ≤3 clicks/taps
Color-coded alert severity meets WCAG 2.1 AA contrast ratios
Wireframes validated by at least 3 ICU clinical staff
Interactive prototype covers all primary nurse workflows end-to-end

plan.description: Design HL7 FHIR R4-aligned data schema for patient vitals, device observations, alert events, and audit logs. Define PostgreSQL schema with TimescaleDB extension for time-series vitals. Include PHI encryption-at-rest design, patient-device linkage tables, and audit trail schema per HIPAA requirements.

payload.encryption: AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit
payload.partitioning: by patient_id and timestamp for query performance

acceptance_criteria: FHIR R4 resource schemas validated against official FHIR StructureDefinitions
TimescaleDB hypertables created for Observation resources with <100ms p99 query for 24h window
PHI fields encrypted at rest using AES-256 with key rotation policy documented
Audit log table captures all PHI access events with user, timestamp, action
Database migration scripts are idempotent and version-controlled

plan.description: Design HL7 FHIR R4 REST API specification: FHIR server endpoints for Patient, Observation, Device, AuditEvent resources; device data ingestion WebSocket endpoints; real-time alert push API (SSE); nurse station notification API. Produce OpenAPI 3.1 spec and FHIR CapabilityStatement.
acceptance_criteria: OpenAPI spec passes lint validation with no errors
FHIR CapabilityStatement lists all supported resource types and interactions
SMART on FHIR authorization flows documented for EHR launch context
Device ingestion endpoint supports HL7 v2 ADT, ORU message parsing
SSE alert stream endpoint defined with reconnection and event-type schema

plan.description: Produce threat model (STRIDE) for the ICU monitoring system covering device communication channels, PHI data at rest and in transit, nurse station integration, and API exposure. Define security architecture: network segmentation, authentication, authorization (RBAC), and audit logging requirements. Produce initial SBOM.
acceptance_criteria: STRIDE threat model covers all identified threat surfaces
Every threat has a documented countermeasure or accepted risk
RBAC matrix defines permissions for all 4 user roles across all API endpoints
SBOM covers all runtime dependencies with CVE status
Security architecture reviewed and approved by security engineer
PHI encryption at rest and in transit
Access audit logging enabled

plan.description: Initialize Design History File (DHF) per FDA 21 CFR Part 820 and ISO 13485. Create Device Design Plan, Risk Management File (linked to step 2 FMEA), Design Input requirements, and Verification & Validation protocol templates. Establish traceability matrix linking requirements → design → test evidence.

payload.risk_management_file: linked to ISO 14971 FMEA from step 2

acceptance_criteria: DHF document structure approved by regulatory specialist
All Design Inputs traceable to stakeholder requirements from BRD
RTM links each requirement to ≥1 verification test case
Risk Management File references FMEA from step 2 with risk control measures
V&V protocol templates reviewed and approved before implementation begins
DHF (Design History File) structure created
V&V protocol drafted

plan.description: Implement device adapter firmware/drivers for bedside device integration. Build HL7 v2 message parser (ADT, ORU, MDM), serial/RS-232 and TCP/IP device communication handlers, and a lightweight RTOS-based polling loop for vital sign monitors. Target embedded gateway hardware (ARM Cortex-M or Linux-based edge device).

payload.target: ARM Cortex-A (Linux edge gateway) or Cortex-M (bare-metal fallback)

acceptance_criteria: HL7 v2 ORU^R01 parser correctly extracts all OBX segment vital sign values
Device communication handler reconnects automatically within 5s of TCP disconnect
All firmware modules have corresponding unit tests with ≥80% line coverage
IEC 62304 software unit documentation completed for each module
Firmware compiles without warnings under -Wall -Wextra for target architecture
IEC 62304 software class documented
SOUP components listed

plan.description: Implement the FHIR R4 server and device data ingestion service. Build: HAPI FHIR server (Java) or Python FHIR server with resource validation, HL7 v2→FHIR R4 transformation pipeline, real-time observation ingestion from device adapters via WebSocket/MQTT, and TimescaleDB persistence layer. Implement SMART on FHIR OAuth2 authorization server integration.

payload.fhir_server: HAPI FHIR (Java) or fhir-kit-client (Node.js) with custom extensions
payload.ingestion: MQTT broker (Mosquitto) + WebSocket bridge for device adapters
payload.transformation: HL7 v2 to FHIR R4 Observation mapping engine
payload.auth: Keycloak with SMART on FHIR plugin
payload.agentic_pattern: multi_agent_parallel — parallel ingestion agents per device type

acceptance_criteria: FHIR server passes HL7 FHIR R4 validation for all supported resource types
HL7 v2 ORU messages transformed to FHIR Observation resources with correct coding (LOINC)
Device data ingestion handles ≥500 concurrent device connections
TimescaleDB writes complete within 50ms p99 for single observation
SMART on FHIR token introspection integrated and rejecting invalid tokens

plan.description: Implement real-time alerting engine and nurse station notification service. Build configurable alert rule engine (vital sign thresholds, device disconnect, data staleness), alert deduplication and suppression logic, escalation policy engine (P1→P2→P3), and nurse station push notification via SSE and HL7 ADT/ACK messaging.

payload.deduplication: sliding 60s window per patient per alert type
payload.escalation: configurable escalation chain with timeout per tier
payload.agentic_pattern: human_in_the_loop — critical alerts require nurse acknowledgment within SLA

acceptance_criteria: Alert triggers within 2 seconds of threshold breach in device data
Duplicate alert suppression prevents >1 alert per patient per rule per 60s window
Escalation to next tier fires automatically if acknowledgment SLA breached
Nurse station SSE stream delivers alert within 500ms of generation
Alert acknowledgment recorded in FHIR AuditEvent resource

plan.description: Implement ICU monitoring dashboard React application. Build: multi-patient grid with real-time vital sign tiles, single-patient detail view with trend charts (Chart.js/Recharts), alert panel with acknowledgment workflow, device status board, nurse station display mode, and SMART on FHIR EHR launch integration. SSE-driven live updates.

payload.state_management: Zustand for alert state, React Query for FHIR data
payload.real_time: SSE EventSource for alert stream, polling fallback
payload.auth: SMART on FHIR launch sequence (EHR context)

acceptance_criteria: Multi-patient grid renders 12-patient view with <100ms initial load on LAN
Vital sign values update in UI within 1 second of backend SSE event
Alert acknowledge modal appears within 200ms of clicking alert
WCAG 2.1 AA audit passes for all primary views
SMART on FHIR EHR launch flow tested against SMART App Launcher

plan.description: Configure SAGE solution YAML files for patient_monitoring_dashboard: project.yaml (solution manifest, active modules, HITL level = strict), prompts.yaml (clinical analyst, alert reviewer, compliance reviewer agent roles), tasks.yaml (alert_review, vitals_analysis, device_health_check task types). Set up agentic coordinator pattern for multi-agent monitoring.
acceptance_criteria: project.yaml validates against SAGE schema with no errors
SAGE backend starts with PROJECT=patient_monitoring_dashboard without errors
All 3 agent roles defined in prompts.yaml with domain-specific system prompts
task_types in tasks.yaml cover all clinical monitoring workflows
HITL gate active for alert_review and compliance_audit_check task types

plan.description: Set up CI/CD pipeline, container orchestration, and monitoring infrastructure. Docker Compose (dev) + Kubernetes (prod) with HIPAA-compliant logging. GitHub Actions CI: lint, unit tests, FHIR validation, SAST scan (Semgrep). Prometheus + Grafana monitoring. Alertmanager integration. Network segmentation for device VLAN isolation.

payload.logging: ELK stack with PHI field masking
payload.network: Device VLAN isolation, mTLS between services

acceptance_criteria: CI pipeline runs on every PR and fails on SAST findings of severity HIGH+
FHIR resource validation runs in CI against HAPI FHIR validator
PHI fields masked/redacted in all log outputs (verified by log audit)
Kubernetes resource limits defined for all services
Grafana dashboard covers ingestion latency, alert delivery latency, device connection count

plan.description: Design and implement hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) test specs for device adapter firmware. Build a device simulator (HL7 v2 message generator) for ventilators, infusion pumps, and vital sign monitors. Write firmware unit tests using Unity/CppUTest. Define HIL test harness for disconnect/reconnect, malformed message, and high-throughput scenarios.

payload.simulator: Python-based HL7 v2 device simulator (generates ADT, ORU, MDM messages)
payload.coverage_target: ≥85% line coverage on firmware adapter modules

acceptance_criteria: Device simulator generates valid HL7 v2 ORU^R01 messages for all 3 device types
Firmware unit test suite passes with ≥85% line coverage
HIL disconnect/reconnect test: adapter recovers and resumes within 5 seconds
Malformed HL7 message test: adapter logs error, does not crash, resumes processing
1000 msg/s flood test: no message loss, queue depth bounded

plan.description: Produce comprehensive QA test plan covering functional, regression, usability, and performance testing. Define test cases for: vital sign data accuracy, alert generation correctness, FHIR resource conformance, multi-user concurrency, and nurse station notification delivery. Establish defect severity classification aligned to patient risk levels.

payload.traceability: each test case linked to RTM requirement
payload.tools: pytest, Playwright (E2E), k6 (load testing), FHIR Touchstone (conformance)

acceptance_criteria: Test plan covers 100% of requirements in RTM
Each test case has explicit pass/fail criteria and expected data values
Defect severity matrix approved by clinical stakeholder and safety engineer
FHIR conformance test suite configured in FHIR Touchstone
Performance test targets defined: <2s alert delivery, <100ms UI update, 500 concurrent users

plan.description: Execute security review and penetration test plan for the ICU monitoring platform. Test: SMART on FHIR token bypass attempts, PHI data leakage in API responses, HL7 injection via device simulator, RBAC privilege escalation, and SSE stream hijacking. Produce finalized SBOM with CVE remediation status.
acceptance_criteria: Zero critical or high CVEs in SBOM without documented remediation or accepted risk
No PHI returned in unauthenticated API responses
SMART on FHIR token bypass tests all fail (auth enforced)
HL7 injection fuzzing: no crashes or unexpected data insertion
Pentest report reviewed by security engineer and all critical findings remediated
PHI encryption at rest and in transit
Access audit logging enabled

plan.description: Execute end-to-end system test suite: full patient admit→monitor→alert→acknowledge→discharge workflow across all device types. Load test: 50 concurrent ICU patients, 3 device types each, sustained 30 minutes. FHIR conformance validation via FHIR Touchstone. Nurse station integration test with real HL7 ACK round-trip.

payload.fhir_conformance: FHIR Touchstone test suite against all resource types

acceptance_criteria: All E2E scenarios pass without errors on staging environment
Load test: zero alert drops, p99 alert delivery ≤2s under 50-patient load
FHIR Touchstone reports 100% pass rate for all mandatory interactions
Nurse station HL7 ACK received within 1s of alert push
System test report signed off by system_tester and QA engineer

plan.description: Complete DHF with all executed V&V evidence: link each test execution record to RTM, attach system test results as Design Verification evidence, produce Design Validation protocol (clinical environment testing), and compile the complete Device Design History File for regulatory submission readiness.

payload.validation_environment: simulated ICU with clinical staff performing acceptance testing

acceptance_criteria: Every requirement in RTM has ≥1 passing test result as evidence
Risk Management Report updated with post-test residual risk assessment
No unmitigated critical risks remain open in FMEA
DHF completeness checklist per 21 CFR 820.30 fully checked
Regulatory specialist confirms DHF is 510(k) submission-ready
DHF (Design History File) structure created
V&V protocol drafted

plan.description: Prepare FDA 510(k) premarket notification package: predicate device comparison, substantial equivalence argument, performance testing summary, software documentation per FDA guidance (Software as a Medical Device), and cybersecurity documentation per FDA 2023 Cybersecurity guidance.
acceptance_criteria: 510(k) package includes all required sections per FDA eSTAR format
Software documentation meets Level C requirements (highest risk class) per FDA guidance
Cybersecurity documentation includes threat model, SBOM, and incident response plan
Substantial equivalence argument references ≥1 cleared predicate device
Submission package reviewed by regulatory specialist before filing
HIPAA risk assessment complete
BAA template prepared

plan.description: Produce operational runbooks, SLA definitions, incident response playbooks, and capacity planning for the ICU monitoring platform. Define SLAs: system uptime (99.9%), alert delivery latency (≤2s), data retention (7 years). Create incident severity levels with on-call escalation paths for clinical safety incidents.

payload.on_call: 24/7 with clinical safety escalation path to biomedical engineering
payload.capacity_planning: 100 concurrent ICU beds per instance, horizontal scaling plan

acceptance_criteria: All runbooks tested in staging environment and steps verified executable
PHI breach incident response playbook meets HIPAA 60-day notification requirement
SLA targets documented and reviewed by clinical IT leadership
Capacity model validated: 100-bed load test passes with defined SLAs
On-call escalation tree reviewed and contacts confirmed

plan.description: Produce training materials for all user roles: ICU nurse quick-start guide (alert acknowledgment, patient view navigation), intensivist clinical workflow guide, biomedical engineer device integration guide, and IT administrator deployment and maintenance guide. Include video scripts for LMS integration.

audience_materials.icu_nurse: alert acknowledgment, patient grid navigation, escalation procedures
audience_materials.intensivist: trend analysis, alert threshold customization, device status review
audience_materials.biomedical_engineer: device adapter configuration, HL7 mapping, connectivity troubleshooting
audience_materials.it_administrator: deployment, user management, SMART on FHIR configuration, backup/restore

acceptance_criteria: Nurse quick-start guide reviewed and approved by ≥2 ICU nurses
All guides reviewed for clinical accuracy by clinical stakeholder
LMS module structure defined with knowledge check questions per section
Video scripts cover all primary workflows shown in UX wireframes
Training materials version-controlled and linked to product version

plan.description: Produce technical documentation: architecture decision records (ADRs), FHIR CapabilityStatement and implementation guide, API reference (generated from OpenAPI spec), developer onboarding README, deployment guide, and IEC 62304 Software Architecture Document.

documents: architecture_decision_records (ADRs for FHIR server choice, DB, auth)

acceptance_criteria: IEC 62304 Software Architecture Document covers all software items and their classification
FHIR Implementation Guide published and passes IG Publisher validation
API reference generated from final OpenAPI spec with all endpoints documented
Developer README allows a new engineer to run the full stack locally in <30 minutes
All ADRs document the rejected alternatives and rationale

flaws: IEC 60601-1-8 (Alarm Systems for Medical Electrical Equipment) is completely absent from the entire plan. This is the primary standard governing medical alarm management — precisely what this product is. Its omission is not a minor gap; it is a foundational regulatory miss that invalidates the alarm engine design and could block FDA clearance.

IEC 62304 software safety class is left as 'Class B or C' (step 3) but this determination gates every subsequent design decision: documentation depth, testing coverage requirements, architecture decomposition, and SOUP management. Deferring this to step 3 while firmware (step 10) assumes ≥80% line coverage is backward. Class C (almost certain for an ICU alerting system) mandates 100% branch coverage of safety-critical units — this plan's 80% line coverage target is flatly non-compliant.

Step 11 has an unresolved architectural fork: 'HAPI FHIR (Java) or fhir-kit-client (Node.js)'. These are not interchangeable options — HAPI FHIR is a full server with its own persistence and validation engine; fhir-kit-client is a REST client library requiring a separate FHIR server. Leaving this unresolved at implementation time means the database schema (step 6), API design (step 7), and DevOps (step 15) could all need rework. This is an ADR that must close before step 6.

No real physical device interoperability testing exists in the plan. Step 16 builds a Python simulator, but Philips IntelliVue, Dräger Evita, Masimo Radical, and GE Carescape all implement HL7 v2 with vendor-specific extensions, non-standard segment ordering, and proprietary OBX identifiers. A simulator will not expose these divergences. Without testing against actual devices or captured production HL7 traffic, the firmware will fail in the first hospital.

The Mosquitto MQTT broker (step 11) is a critical single point of failure for all device data ingestion. In an ICU, broker downtime means loss of all real-time patient monitoring. No clustering, HA configuration, or broker-level persistence is specified. A single Mosquitto instance does not meet the 99.9% SLA defined in step 22.

Step 10 firmware target is 'ARM Cortex-A or Cortex-M' — a hardware architecture decision left open during implementation. Bare-metal FreeRTOS and Yocto Linux are fundamentally different development environments with different toolchains, SOUP lists, and IEC 62304 documentation requirements. This must be resolved before any firmware is written.

The HL7 v2 → FHIR R4 transformation (step 11) is treated as a 'mapping engine' — a single-step implementation task. In reality this is a 6-12 week effort involving LOINC code mapping tables, handling missing/malformed segments, managing vendor-specific PV1/OBX variants, and building a test corpus from real device traffic. The plan has no dedicated step for this and no acceptance criterion for mapping coverage across device types.

Step 14 introduces SAGE AI agent roles ('alert_reviewer', 'clinical_analyst') that could influence clinical alert decisions. If these agents surface clinical recommendations that affect care, they are themselves SaMD requiring separate FDA clearance. The plan neither flags this regulatory risk nor constrains agent outputs to administrative/operational scope only.

Design Validation (actual clinical environment testing with clinical staff on patient-representative scenarios) is referenced in step 20 as a 'protocol to be produced' but never executed as a plan step. FDA 21 CFR 820.30(g) requires Design Validation evidence, not just a protocol. The plan ends with a protocol draft, not executed validation evidence.

mTLS for device communications (step 8) is specified as a control, but legacy ICU devices — the majority of installed base — do not support TLS at all. RS-232 serial devices and older TCP/IP stacks cannot participate in mTLS. The plan has no fallback architecture (e.g., TLS-terminating gateway proxy at the device interface layer) and no acceptance criterion for how non-TLS devices are handled.

Step 22 defines an RTO of 15 minutes but no DR test is in the plan. Runbook documentation does not prove RTO achievability. The load test (step 19) is scoped to 50 patients under normal conditions, not failover scenarios. A 15-minute RTO for an ICU system requires tested, measured failover — not documented intent.

Missing: physiological plausibility validation on ingested data. A SpO2 reading of 150%, heart rate of 500 bpm, or arterial BP of 300/200 mmHg from a malfunctioning or mis-configured device will pass through the ingestion pipeline, be stored, and trigger alerts. No range-gate, calibration-drift detection, or implausibility flagging exists in the design.
suggestions: Add a dedicated step 2.5 (before firmware design) to resolve the IEC 62304 software safety class and produce the Software Development Plan. Gate all subsequent development steps on this determination. Expect Class C — design for it from the start.

Add IEC 60601-1-8 to the regulatory mapping in step 3 and create a dedicated alarm management design step between steps 12 and 13 covering: alarm prioritization taxonomy (high/medium/low/advisory), alarm signal characteristics, alarm condition persistence logic, and nurse override/silence policies. This is not optional for a product in this category.

Replace the Mosquitto single broker with a clustered MQTT setup (EMQX cluster or HiveMQ with HA) or use a Kafka-backed ingestion pipeline for device data, with MQTT as the edge protocol only. Document the HA architecture explicitly before step 11.

Capture real HL7 traffic from at least one target device type (even in a lab environment) before implementing the transformation engine. Create a dedicated 'Device Interoperability Spike' step between steps 10 and 11 with the deliverable of a validated HL7 corpus for each device type.

Add a step for hospital IT infrastructure dependency resolution: VLAN provisioning approval, network access to device ports, firewall rule changes, and integration engine assessment (Mirth Connect/Rhapsody/Corepoint). In most hospitals this takes 4-12 weeks and cannot run in parallel with firmware development.

Constrain SAGE agent roles (step 14) to administrative and operational tasks only (alert routing, shift handover summaries, compliance check status). Add explicit documentation that agent outputs are not clinical decision support and are not used in direct patient care decisions. Alternatively, scope out AI agent functionality until post-clearance.

Add a time synchronization design section to step 6/7: NTP/PTP sync requirements for device adapters, handling of late-arriving or out-of-order messages, and timestamp normalization policy. Without this, trend charts and alert timing will be unreliable when device clocks drift.

Add firmware SOUP (Software of Unknown Provenance) management per IEC 62304 Section 8 as an explicit deliverable in step 10. Each third-party library used in firmware must have a documented version, known anomalies list, and regression test evidence. This is an audit finding target.

Add FDA 2023 Cybersecurity post-market requirements to step 21: ongoing SBOM monitoring process, coordinated vulnerability disclosure policy, and software update mechanism documentation. The 2023 guidance makes these mandatory for submissions.
missing: IEC 60601-1-8 alarm management standard — entirely absent, directly applicable
Dedicated IEC 62304 Software Safety Classification step with gating logic before design begins
Real device interoperability testing step (not simulator-only) with vendor-specific HL7 corpus
Hospital IT/infrastructure dependency resolution step (VLAN, firewall, integration engine assessment)
HL7 Clinical Integration Engine (Mirth/Rhapsody) assessment — most hospitals route all HL7 through a CIE, bypassing the plan's assumed direct device connectivity
ADT feed from EHR (Epic/Cerner) for patient admission/discharge/transfer synchronization — without this, patient-device linkage depends on manual entry
Time synchronization design: NTP/PTP policy for device adapters, clock skew handling in data pipeline
Physiological plausibility validation layer on ingested observations
Joint Commission NPSG 06.01.01 alarm management requirements (US hospital accreditation standard)
FDA post-market cybersecurity surveillance program (required by 2023 guidance for 510(k) submissions)
Executed Design Validation (clinical environment) — plan produces protocol but never executes it
SOUP list and anomaly tracking per IEC 62304 Section 8 for all firmware and backend components
DR failover test demonstrating 15-minute RTO is achievable
Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure (CVD) program documentation
Architectural Decision Record closing the HAPI FHIR vs. alternative FHIR server selection before step 6
security_risks: SSE stream reconnection (step 12/13): if the EventSource reconnects without re-authenticating the OAuth token, an expired or revoked token could maintain an active PHI stream. The plan does not specify token re-validation on SSE reconnect.

WebSocket device ingestion endpoint (step 7/11): device-to-server WebSocket authentication relies on mTLS, which legacy devices cannot perform. If the fallback for non-mTLS devices uses API key or IP allowlisting, those controls are not specified and are typically weak.

HL7 v2 injection: the fuzzing test in step 18 is correct to include, but the HL7 parser in step 10/11 must also be validated for buffer overflow and memory safety — particularly if the C-based HL7 v2 parser processes untrusted device input over TCP. This is a remote code execution surface on a medical device gateway.

Nurse station webhook endpoint: step 12 lists 'REST webhook' as a nurse station protocol. Incoming webhooks without HMAC signature validation are trivially spoofable and could inject false alert acknowledgments, creating a patient safety risk (alert marked acknowledged when it was not).

PHI in Prometheus/Grafana metrics: step 15 uses Prometheus for monitoring. If any metric labels include patient identifiers (e.g., per-patient alert counts), PHI will be present in the metrics endpoint. This is a common HIPAA violation in observability stacks.

ELK stack PHI masking (step 15): 'PHI field masking' is listed as a requirement but the implementation is undefined. Log shipping pipelines frequently have timing gaps where unmasked PHI appears in intermediate buffers (Logstash pipeline, Elasticsearch indexing queue) before masking is applied.

Keycloak SMART on FHIR plugin maturity: the Keycloak SMART on FHIR community plugin has known gaps in the EHR launch context flow and is not officially supported by Keycloak. Production ICU use should evaluate a purpose-built SMART authorization server (e.g., Inferno-compatible) instead.

Admin console threat surface (step 8): the RBAC matrix covers nurse/intensivist/admin/biomedical roles but does not specify what the 'admin' role can access. Unrestricted admin access to PHI query endpoints, FHIR history, and audit logs is a HIPAA minimum-necessary violation risk.
final_review.summary: This plan demonstrates genuine regulatory sophistication — the DHF structure, ISO 14971 FMEA, HIPAA controls, and FHIR R4 alignment are handled with appropriate depth, and the 24-step sequencing correctly front-loads compliance artifacts before implementation. However, it has one disqualifying omission (IEC 60601-1-8 for alarm management) and three critical gaps that would cause production failures or regulatory rejection: the IEC 62304 safety class is unresolved at the point where it governs firmware coverage requirements, the FHIR server architecture is left as an open fork through implementation, and real device interoperability testing is absent in favor of a simulator that will not surface vendor-specific HL7 deviations. The MQTT single-broker design, legacy device mTLS gap, and missing Design Validation execution (protocol only, no evidence) are additional showstoppers for a 510(k) submission. For a regulated ICU monitoring SaMD, this plan is solid preliminary work that needs a targeted rework pass on alarm standards compliance, safety class gating, infrastructure HA, and real-device testing before implementation begins. Current state: suitable for design review and stakeholder alignment, not ready to gate implementation start.

FDA 21 CFR 820.30(g) requires Design Validation evidence, not just a protocol. The plan ends with a protocol draft, not executed validation evidence.

mTLS for device communications (step 8) is specified as a control, but legacy ICU devices — the majority of installed base — do not support TLS at all. RS-232 serial devices and older TCP/IP stacks cannot participate in mTLS. The plan has no fallback architecture (e.g., TLS-terminating gateway proxy at the device interface layer) and no acceptance criterion for how non-TLS devices are handled.

Step 22 defines an RTO of 15 minutes but no DR test is in the plan. Runbook documentation does not prove RTO achievability. The load test (step 19) is scoped to 50 patients under normal conditions, not failover scenarios. A 15-minute RTO for an ICU system requires tested, measured failover — not documented intent.

Missing: physiological plausibility validation on ingested data. A SpO2 reading of 150%, heart rate of 500 bpm, or arterial BP of 300/200 mmHg from a malfunctioning or mis-configured device will pass through the ingestion pipeline, be stored, and trigger alerts. No range-gate, calibration-drift detection, or implausibility flagging exists in the design.
suggestions: Add a dedicated step 2.5 (before firmware design) to resolve the IEC 62304 software safety class and produce the Software Development Plan. Gate all subsequent development steps on this determination. Expect Class C — design for it from the start.

Add IEC 60601-1-8 to the regulatory mapping in step 3 and create a dedicated alarm management design step between steps 12 and 13 covering: alarm prioritization taxonomy (high/medium/low/advisory), alarm signal characteristics, alarm condition persistence logic, and nurse override/silence policies. This is not optional for a product in this category.

Replace the Mosquitto single broker with a clustered MQTT setup (EMQX cluster or HiveMQ with HA) or use a Kafka-backed ingestion pipeline for device data, with MQTT as the edge protocol only. Document the HA architecture explicitly before step 11.

Capture real HL7 traffic from at least one target device type (even in a lab environment) before implementing the transformation engine. Create a dedicated 'Device Interoperability Spike' step between steps 10 and 11 with the deliverable of a validated HL7 corpus for each device type.

Add a step for hospital IT infrastructure dependency resolution: VLAN provisioning approval, network access to device ports, firewall rule changes, and integration engine assessment (Mirth Connect/Rhapsody/Corepoint). In most hospitals this takes 4-12 weeks and cannot run in parallel with firmware development.

Constrain SAGE agent roles (step 14) to administrative and operational tasks only (alert routing, shift handover summaries, compliance check status). Add explicit documentation that agent outputs are not clinical decision support and are not used in direct patient care decisions. Alternatively, scope out AI agent functionality until post-clearance.

Add a time synchronization design section to step 6/7: NTP/PTP sync requirements for device adapters, handling of late-arriving or out-of-order messages, and timestamp normalization policy. Without this, trend charts and alert timing will be unreliable when device clocks drift.

Add firmware SOUP (Software of Unknown Provenance) management per IEC 62304 Section 8 as an explicit deliverable in step 10. Each third-party library used in firmware must have a documented version, known anomalies list, and regression test evidence. This is an audit finding target.

Add FDA 2023 Cybersecurity post-market requirements to step 21: ongoing SBOM monitoring process, coordinated vulnerability disclosure policy, and software update mechanism documentation. The 2023 guidance makes these mandatory for submissions.
missing: IEC 60601-1-8 alarm management standard — entirely absent, directly applicable
Dedicated IEC 62304 Software Safety Classification step with gating logic before design begins
Real device interoperability testing step (not simulator-only) with vendor-specific HL7 corpus
Hospital IT/infrastructure dependency resolution step (VLAN, firewall, integration engine assessment)
HL7 Clinical Integration Engine (Mirth/Rhapsody) assessment — most hospitals route all HL7 through a CIE, bypassing the plan's assumed direct device connectivity
ADT feed from EHR (Epic/Cerner) for patient admission/discharge/transfer synchronization — without this, patient-device linkage depends on manual entry
Time synchronization design: NTP/PTP policy for device adapters, clock skew handling in data pipeline
Physiological plausibility validation layer on ingested observations
Joint Commission NPSG 06.01.01 alarm management requirements (US hospital accreditation standard)
FDA post-market cybersecurity surveillance program (required by 2023 guidance for 510(k) submissions)
Executed Design Validation (clinical environment) — plan produces protocol but never executes it
SOUP list and anomaly tracking per IEC 62304 Section 8 for all firmware and backend components
DR failover test demonstrating 15-minute RTO is achievable
Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure (CVD) program documentation
Architectural Decision Record closing the HAPI FHIR vs. alternative FHIR server selection before step 6
security_risks: SSE stream reconnection (step 12/13): if the EventSource reconnects without re-authenticating the OAuth token, an expired or revoked token could maintain an active PHI stream. The plan does not specify token re-validation on SSE reconnect.

WebSocket device ingestion endpoint (step 7/11): device-to-server WebSocket authentication relies on mTLS, which legacy devices cannot perform. If the fallback for non-mTLS devices uses API key or IP allowlisting, those controls are not specified and are typically weak.

HL7 v2 injection: the fuzzing test in step 18 is correct to include, but the HL7 parser in step 10/11 must also be validated for buffer overflow and memory safety — particularly if the C-based HL7 v2 parser processes untrusted device input over TCP. This is a remote code execution surface on a medical device gateway.

Nurse station webhook endpoint: step 12 lists 'REST webhook' as a nurse station protocol. Incoming webhooks without HMAC signature validation are trivially spoofable and could inject false alert acknowledgments, creating a patient safety risk (alert marked acknowledged when it was not).

PHI in Prometheus/Grafana metrics: step 15 uses Prometheus for monitoring. If any metric labels include patient identifiers (e.g., per-patient alert counts), PHI will be present in the metrics endpoint. This is a common HIPAA violation in observability stacks.

ELK stack PHI masking (step 15): 'PHI field masking' is listed as a requirement but the implementation is undefined. Log shipping pipelines frequently have timing gaps where unmasked PHI appears in intermediate buffers (Logstash pipeline, Elasticsearch indexing queue) before masking is applied.

Keycloak SMART on FHIR plugin maturity: the Keycloak SMART on FHIR community plugin has known gaps in the EHR launch context flow and is not officially supported by Keycloak. Production ICU use should evaluate a purpose-built SMART authorization server (e.g., Inferno-compatible) instead.

Admin console threat surface (step 8): the RBAC matrix covers nurse/intensivist/admin/biomedical roles but does not specify what the 'admin' role can access. Unrestricted admin access to PHI query endpoints, FHIR history, and audit logs is a HIPAA minimum-necessary violation risk.
history.summary: This plan demonstrates genuine regulatory sophistication — the DHF structure, ISO 14971 FMEA, HIPAA controls, and FHIR R4 alignment are handled with appropriate depth, and the 24-step sequencing correctly front-loads compliance artifacts before implementation. However, it has one disqualifying omission (IEC 60601-1-8 for alarm management) and three critical gaps that would cause production failures or regulatory rejection: the IEC 62304 safety class is unresolved at the point where it governs firmware coverage requirements, the FHIR server architecture is left as an open fork through implementation, and real device interoperability testing is absent in favor of a simulator that will not surface vendor-specific HL7 deviations. The MQTT single-broker design, legacy device mTLS gap, and missing Design Validation execution (protocol only, no evidence) are additional showstoppers for a 510(k) submission. For a regulated ICU monitoring SaMD, this plan is solid preliminary work that needs a targeted rework pass on alarm standards compliance, safety class gating, infrastructure HA, and real-device testing before implementation begins. Current state: suitable for design review and stakeholder alignment, not ready to gate implementation start.
